Genomic Imprinting Analysis in Mimulus nudatus Hybrid Endosperm

Elen Oneal

April 06, 2026

Introduction

Genomic imprinting is an epigenetic phenomenon in which gene expression is biased toward one parental allele. In flowering plants, imprinting is largely restricted to the endosperm, a triploid nutritive tissue that supports embryo development. Because the endosperm is formed by fusion of the diploid maternal central cell with a haploid paternal gamete, most endosperm-expressed genes conform to an expected 2:1 maternal:paternal expression ratio. Imprinted genes deviate from this expectation. Maternally expressed genes (MEGs) show maternal bias, while paternally expressed genes (PEGs) show paternal bias.

Imprinting is theorized to result from genomic conflict between maternal and paternal genomes over resource allocation to offspring. Disruption in imprinting and the subsequent disruption in endosperm tissue development can lead to hybrid seed inviability, and is thought to be an important factor in the emergence of reproductive isolation (speciation) flowering plants. If so, then imprinted genes may play a causal role in the emergence of barriers to hybridization between closely related species. This project is part of a larger research study that combines rna sequencing of endosperm transcriptomes with QTL mapping in reciprocal hybrid backcross populations to identify and characterize genomic loci contributing to hybrid seed inviability.

Here we characterize genomic imprinting in hybrid endosperm from reciprocal crosses within the Mimulus guttatus species complex, focusing on crosses between M. nudatus inbred lines DHRO22 and GMR2. We use allele-specific expression (ASE) analysis to identify MEGs and PEGs, leveraging parent-of-origin SNPs identified from parental DNA-seq data.

Study Design

Reciprocal crosses were performed between two M. nudatus inbred lines:

  • DHRO22 × GMR2 — DHRO22 as maternal parent (3 replicates)
  • GMR2 × DHRO22 — GMR2 as maternal parent (4 replicates)

RNA-seq libraries were generated from hybrid endosperm tissue. Reads were aligned to the M. guttatus IM767 v2 reference genome using STAR. Allele-specific read counts were quantified at parent-of-origin SNPs identified from parental DNA-seq using BWA and bcftools. Imprinting was tested using a likelihood ratio test in edgeR, with cross direction as a covariate to control for maternal effects.

In addition to these samples, we also sequenced hybrid endosperms in multiple M. guttatus crosses. Finally, we sequenced whole fruits of 2 M. guttatus and 3 M. nudatus fruits. These samples are included for visualization purposes only.

MDS Analysis

We first examined sample clustering using multidimensional scaling (MDS) to assess overall sample quality and verify that samples cluster by species and cross direction.

The first MDS plot includes all samples RNA sequenced in this study.

We then restrict our analysis to the M. nudatus hybrid endosperm.

We next examined the overlap between genes expressed in M. nudatus endosperm and genes with informative parent-of-origin SNPs, since only genes in both sets can be tested for imprinting.

Load files with gene information: mimulus/arabidopsis orthologs, synonyms for arabidopsis genes, defline for arabidopsis genes, and coordinates for genes in IM767 v2 reference genome.

Load allelic counts for each sample in the hybrid endosperm RNA sequencing experiment. Perform imprinting analysis using edgeR. We use edgeR to fit a negative binomial GLM incorporating both read source (maternal vs paternal) and cross direction as covariates. A likelihood ratio test (LRT) identified genes with significant parent-of-origin biased expression while controlling for cross direction effects, with FDR correction using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.

## MEGs: 53
## PEGs: 273
## Mean maternal proportion MEGs: 0.914
## Mean maternal proportion PEGs: 0.210
Summary of imprinting analysis in M. nudatus hybrid endosperm
Metric Value

Paternally expressed genes (PEGs) 273
Mean maternal proportion in MEGs 91.4%
